title: Listening - Part 1
description: You will hear three different extracts. For questions 1–6, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best.
instructions: You will hear three different extracts. For questions 1–6, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear. There are two questions for each extract.

extracts.context: You hear two colleagues discussing a professional development course they are considering.
extracts.audio_script: Man: So, are you going to sign up for that 'Leadership in the Digital Age' seminar? The brochure makes it sound like the holy grail of management training. || Woman: I’m on the fence. It’s a significant investment, both in terms of time and the departmental budget. || Man: True, but [1]what really draws me to it is the calibre of the guest speakers they’ve lined up[/1]. Having the former CEO of that tech giant giving a workshop is a rare opportunity. || Woman: I see your point. However, [2]I’m slightly concerned that the curriculum seems a bit light on actual data analysis[/2]. It’s all very well talking about 'vision' and 'culture', but I need something I can quantify. || Man: I suppose it depends on what you’re looking for. I’m more interested in the networking side of things, to be honest. || Woman: Well, if you’re going for the contacts, it’s probably worth it. I might look for something more technical.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: So, are you going to sign up for that 'Leadership in the Digital Age' seminar? The brochure makes it sound like the holy grail of management training.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I’m on the fence. It’s a significant investment, both in terms of time and the departmental budget.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: True, but what really draws me to it is the calibre of the guest speakers they’ve lined up. Having the former CEO of that tech giant giving a workshop is a rare opportunity.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I see your point. However, I’m slightly concerned that the curriculum seems a bit light on actual data analysis. It’s all very well talking about 'vision' and 'culture', but I need something I can quantify.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I suppose it depends on what you’re looking for. I’m more interested in the networking side of things, to be honest.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Well, if you’re going for the contacts, it’s probably worth it. I might look for something more technical.

questions.question: What is the man's primary reason for wanting to attend the seminar?

options.A: to improve his practical management skills
options.B: to learn from highly experienced individuals
options.C: to enhance his CV for future promotions

questions.explanation: B is correct because he emphasizes the 'calibre of the guest speakers' as the main draw [1].

questions.question: The woman expresses doubt about the seminar's

options.C: lack of focus on measurable skills.

questions.explanation: C is supported by her concern that the course is 'light on actual data analysis' and too focused on abstract concepts [2].

extracts.context: You hear two friends discussing a new video game that has become a global phenomenon.
extracts.audio_script: Man: I finally sat down and played 'Neon Frontier' last night. I can see why everyone is obsessed with the world-building; it’s incredibly detailed. || Woman: It’s gorgeous, isn't it? But [3]I found the core gameplay loop a bit repetitive after the first few hours[/3]. It’s basically just running errands for non-player characters. || Man: I actually find that quite relaxing! It’s the perfect way to unwind after a stressful day. Plus, the soundtrack is phenomenal. || Woman: True, the music is a highlight. But [4]don't you think the ending felt a bit rushed?[/4] It’s like they spent years on the first two acts and then realized they had to finish the story in a weekend. || Man: I agree, it did leave a few too many loose ends. I suspect they’re holding back the resolution for a paid expansion pack later this year. || Woman: Typical. I’d rather have a complete experience from the start.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I finally sat down and played 'Neon Frontier' last night. I can see why everyone is obsessed with the world-building; it’s incredibly detailed.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: It’s gorgeous, isn't it? But I found the core gameplay loop a bit repetitive after the first few hours. It’s basically just running errands for non-player characters.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I actually find that quite relaxing! It’s the perfect way to unwind after a stressful day. Plus, the soundtrack is phenomenal.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: True, the music is a highlight. But don't you think the ending felt a bit rushed? It’s like they spent years on the first two acts and then realized they had to finish the story in a weekend.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I agree, it did leave a few too many loose ends. I suspect they’re holding back the resolution for a paid expansion pack later this year.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Typical. I’d rather have a complete experience from the start.

questions.question: What was the woman's opinion of the gameplay?

options.C: She felt it was too difficult for beginners.

questions.explanation: B is supported by her comment that the gameplay loop was 'repetitive' [3].

questions.question: Both speakers agree that the game's conclusion

options.B: failed to provide a satisfying wrap-up.
options.C: was the most technically impressive part.

questions.explanation: B is correct because both agree the ending felt 'rushed' and left 'too many loose ends' [4].

extracts.context: You hear two people discussing a museum exhibition about the history of fashion.
extracts.audio_script: Woman: That exhibition on Victorian formal wear was fascinating. The level of craftsmanship in those corsets was staggering, even if the actual garments looked incredibly painful to wear. || Man: It was eye-opening. [5]What struck me was how much social status was dictated by the sheer impracticality of the clothing[/5]. If you can't lift your arms, you’re clearly not doing manual labour. || Woman: Exactly. It’s a visual shorthand for class. But [6]I was a bit disappointed that the exhibition didn't explore the lives of the people who actually made the clothes[/6]. It was all about the wearers, not the makers. || Man: That’s a fair point. A few stories from the sweatshops would have provided some much-needed perspective on the human cost of that 'elegance'. || Woman: It felt a bit like they were glorifying the past without acknowledging the reality of the production.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: That exhibition on Victorian formal wear was fascinating. The level of craftsmanship in those corsets was staggering, even if the actual garments looked incredibly painful to wear.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: It was eye-opening. What struck me was how much social status was dictated by the sheer impracticality of the clothing. If you can't lift your arms, you’re clearly not doing manual labour.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Exactly. It’s a visual shorthand for class. But I was a bit disappointed that the exhibition didn't explore the lives of the people who actually made the clothes. It was all about the wearers, not the makers.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: That’s a fair point. A few stories from the sweatshops would have provided some much-needed perspective on the human cost of that 'elegance'.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: It felt a bit like they were glorifying the past without acknowledging the reality of the production.

questions.question: What did the man find most interesting about the clothing on display?

options.A: the materials used in their construction
options.B: the way they reflected societal hierarchies
options.C: the evolution of style over the century

questions.explanation: B is supported by his observation that status was linked to the 'impracticality of the clothing' [5].

questions.question: What was the woman's main criticism of the exhibition?

options.A: It lacked a critical view of the garment industry.
options.B: It was too focused on one specific historical period.
options.C: The displays were poorly lit and hard to see.

questions.explanation: A is correct because she was disappointed that it 'didn't explore the lives of the people who actually made the clothes' [6].
